theme: Post-Crash Earnings Season Recovery — Momentum Continuation vs. Reversal

macro_context.backdrop: Period immediately follows the Aug 4-5 Japan carry trade unwind shock. Nikkei -12.5% on Aug 5, VIX spiked to 65 (pandemic-era levels). S&P 500 dropped ~6% in 3 days. By Aug 6, market is in active recovery mode — the question is: dip-buy strength vs. dead-cat bounce.
macro_context.earnings_season_state: Q2 2025 earnings season in full swing. By mid-August, ~66% of S&P 500 had reported. 82% beat EPS (above 5Y avg of 78%). Revenue beats moderate. Market entered season with lowered bar from tariff uncertainty. Beat rate high but guidance mattered more than results.
macro_context.fed_backdrop: September rate cut probability 88.9% post-July CPI (Aug 12 release). Core CPI +3.1% YoY, tariff effects modest. Dual mandate shifting toward labor market focus. Rate cut trajectory: Sept → Oct (67% prob) → Dec. This underpins risk-on recovery.
macro_context.tariff_context: Trump tariff pause extended through Aug 7. New tariffs went live Aug 7 (Thursday). Initial market reaction: up, then fade on implementation. Tariff impact on inflation modest in July data — key positive for rate cut narrative.
key_earnings_aug6: Disney (DIS) — EPS beat $1.61 vs $1.45, revenue MISS $23.65B vs est; theme parks strong; DIS stock gapped DOWN post-report
Uber (UBER) — Q2 beat: rev $12.65B vs $12.46B est, $20B buyback announced; stock DOWN on 'sell the news' (YTD +50%)
Airbnb (ABNB) — Q2 beat: rev $3.1B vs $3.03B est, EPS $1.03 beat; $6B buyback; stock CRASHED -7.5% day-after (guidance nuance)
Shopify (SHOP) — MASSIVE beat: rev +31%, NI +430%, GMV $88B; stock GAPPED UP $127→$155 (+22%)
key_earnings_aug13: Cisco (CSCO) — Q4 beat: EPS $0.99 vs $0.98, rev $14.7B vs $14.62B; AI orders >$800M Q4; stock FELL 1.42% — 'sell the news' + inline FY26 guidance
macro_context.july_cpi_aug12: July CPI +0.2% MoM, +2.7% YoY. Core +0.3%/+3.1%. Released Aug 12. Market rally of 2-3% followed. September cut odds surged to 88.9%. Critical positive catalyst for the period.
macro_context.goldrising: GLD moved from $311 (Aug 5) to $325+ (Sep 2), gold in sustained uptrend — flight to quality/inflation hedge persisting despite risk recovery

trades.rationale: Shopify reported Q2 after close Aug 5: revenue +31% YoY to $2.68B, net income +430% to $906M, GMV $88B (+31%). Massive beat across all metrics. Stock was at $127 pre-earnings. Gap-up opening expected. Post-VIX-65 recovery context — beaten-down high-quality compounder with real fundamentals. Earnings momentum stocks lead first leg of recovery.
trades.catalyst: Q2 2025 earnings massive beat; net income +430%; 8 consecutive quarters of double-digit FCF margins; B2B GMV +101%
trades.what_happened: SHOP gapped up 22% to $155 on Aug 6 as expected, but momentum stalled immediately. The gap-fill began the next day (-2.5%) and continued. The post-earnings sellers overwhelmed buyers as valuation stretched (SHOP was already richly valued at ~10x sales). By Aug 19 fell to $139 (-10% from entry). The earnings beat was priced in too aggressively at open. Classic 'gap and trap' — the move happened before entry was possible at a safe price.
trades.lesson: Chasing gap-ups on earnings day is a trap when valuation is stretched and the move is 20%+. Better entry: wait for gap-fill consolidation (SHOP stabilized ~$140-148 range post-gap). The quality of the beat was real but timing/entry destroyed the trade.

trades.rationale: Super Micro Computer reported Q4 FY2025 on Aug 5 after close: EPS $0.41 vs $0.44 est (MISS), revenue $5.8B vs $5.96B est (MISS). Q1 FY2026 guidance: rev $6-7B vs $6.6B est (MISS). Gross margins compressed due to hyperscale GPU rack mix. SMCI had been a battleground stock plagued by accounting concerns. Double miss + guidance miss in a rising-competition environment (DELL, HPE eating its lunch). Pre-crash price $57.26, stock already down from $100+ highs.
trades.catalyst: Q4 2025 double miss; Q1 guidance below consensus; margin compression from low-margin hyperscale mix; continued competition from DELL/HPE in AI server market
trades.what_happened: SMCI opened down -18% on Aug 6 (from $57.26 to ~$46.79) — the crash already happened before entry. Stock then drifted lower from $46.79 to $44.36 over 14 days, a further 5% decline. The structural bear case (competition, margin compression, accounting overhang) kept selling pressure but the initial collapse had been absorbed. Should have entered the short before earnings or avoided.
trades.lesson: The 'best' short entry was PRE-earnings when risk/reward was optimal. Post-gap shorts on fundamentally weak names have limited further downside if the single-day crash is massive. SMCI did continue lower but the fat pitch was gone. For shorts, need to be positioned BEFORE the catalyst fires, not after.

trades.rationale: Tesla at $319 on Aug 6, bouncing off Aug 5 crash low of $308. Broader market recovering from VIX-65 spike. TSLA had no negative earnings catalyst (Q2 results already reported in July). Post-carry-trade-unwind recovery trades favor high-beta names. Fed rate cut narrative (Sept 88.9%) supportive of growth/speculative. FSD/Cybercab narrative starting to build into fall. No specific near-term short thesis.
trades.catalyst: Post-VIX spike recovery in high-beta; market breadth expanding; Fed cut on horizon; no negative catalyst; autonomy narrative building
trades.what_happened: TSLA rallied strongly from $319 → $340+ by Aug 11-12, then consolidated briefly before another leg up. By Aug 26, reached $351.67. The recovery trade worked cleanly. TSLA's high-beta nature amplified the market recovery. Interestingly, TSLA continued to rally MUCH harder after Sep 5 (autonomy day catalysts), reaching $426 by Sep 19. Exiting at $351 captured a solid move but left a lot on the table.
trades.lesson: High-beta recovery plays in a V-shaped bounce work well when macro (Fed cut) is tailwind. TSLA specifically had option-value from autonomy story that wasn't fully priced. Should have sized this larger and held through September given clear catalyst path (Cybercab/FSD updates).

trades.rationale: Uber beat Q2 estimates and announced $20B buyback on Aug 6. However stock was DOWN -0.2% on the day despite strong results (YTD already +50%, 'sell the news'). Market was recovering post-crash. Thesis: the buyback provides a floor; the operational beat (trips +18%, EBITDA +35%) shows durable growth; $20B buyback on a ~$95B market cap is massive (~21%). Stock will recover once initial profit-taking absorbed.
trades.catalyst: $20B stock buyback (21% of market cap); Q2 rev beat $12.65B vs $12.46B; EBITDA $2.12B +35% YoY; trips +18% YoY
trades.what_happened: UBER initially sold off post-earnings (profit taking on +50% YTD stock). But the buyback floor and operational quality drove a steady grind higher: $89 → $90 → $92 → $95 → $96+. By Aug 19, hit $95.32, continued to $96.53 by Aug 26. No major acceleration but clean upward drift. The buyback authorization acted as a constant bid.
trades.lesson: Massive buybacks on quality operators with momentum businesses provide excellent risk/reward. 'Sell the news' on earnings creates low-risk entry points when the underlying business is compounding. UBER's unit economics were improving quarter over quarter — this was a real business, not a story stock.

trades.rationale: AMD reported Q2 on Aug 5: revenue $7.7B (+32% YoY, +3.6% beat), strong data center performance. EPS slightly missed due to $800M MI308/China export restriction inventory charge — one-time, not structural. Stock dropped -6% on Aug 6 to $163 from $174 pre-earnings (despite revenue beat). The sell-off was an overreaction to a non-recurring charge. Data center CPU (EPYC) and GPU pipeline strong. MI355 shipping with positive demand signals. Recovery trade post-Aug-5 crash AND post-earnings overreaction creates double-entry signal.
trades.catalyst: Post-earnings overreaction to one-time inventory charge; Q2 revenue +32% YoY beat; EPYC server CPU market share gains; strong Q3 guidance $8.7B; MI355 now shipping
trades.what_happened: AMD recovered from $163 (post-earnings low on Aug 6) to $172-184 range by Aug 12-13, with a notable spike to $184.42 on Aug 13. But failed to sustain — fell back below $180 by Aug 14-15, then continued lower. By Aug 19 at $166.55, Aug 27 at $167.13. The initial recovery worked but AMD couldn't maintain momentum. The China export restriction narrative weighed persistently. Competitors NVDA/INTC dynamic kept pressure. The thesis (one-time charge) was right but macro selling pressure in late Aug overwhelmed.
trades.lesson: Correct earnings narrative doesn't always translate to correct trade within the holding window. AMD's structural story was right (EPYC gains, MI355 shipping) but the 2-week window hit a rough patch. Position sizing and conviction-based holding period matters. Also: AMD's China exposure overhang is a persistent discount, not just one quarter.

trades.rationale: Palantir at $187 on Aug 12 — massive run from $154 (post-earnings Aug 4-5) to $187 in 7 trading days (+21%). Q2 was genuinely strong (rev $1B, US commercial +93%, Rule of 40 = 94%) but the stock had already priced in perfection. At $187, PLTR traded at ~90x forward earnings and ~40x NTM revenue. 'Buy the rumor sell the news' dynamic — stock front-ran earnings by 50%+ YTD. Post-crash bounce amplified the move. Now overbought, valuation extreme, insider selling risk. Short the exhaustion.
trades.catalyst: Post-earnings momentum exhaustion; extreme valuation ($187 = ~90x fwd EPS); stock already +50%+ YTD before earnings; VIX normalization reduces FOMO bid; no near-term catalyst to sustain rally
trades.what_happened: PLTR peaked at $187 on Aug 12 and then entered sustained selling: $187 → $184 → $181 → $177 → $174 → $157 by Aug 19. A -15.5% move in 7 days. The selling was broad-based — as VIX normalized and risk appetite became more selective, high-multiple momentum names with no near-term catalyst were sold. By Sep 2, PLTR was at $157 (-16%). The valuation overhang was a real anchor. The earnings momentum had fully dissipated 8 days after reporting.
trades.lesson: Short high-multiple AI names post-earnings when: (1) stock ran 20%+ in 2 weeks heading into report; (2) beat was genuine but 'already known'; (3) no next catalyst visible within 6-8 weeks; (4) broader market transitioning from FOMO back to valuation discipline. PLTR is a great company that will likely face this dynamic repeatedly — 90x earnings is not sustainable during risk-off rotations.

trades.rationale: Gold at $308 on Aug 12. CPI report due Aug 12: expected +2.7% YoY, core +3.1%. If in-line or hotter: gold wins (inflation hedge). If cooler: September rate cut bets rise → dollar weakens → gold also wins. Double-win setup. Additionally: post-VIX-65 spike, geopolitical uncertainty elevated, central bank buying sustained. Gold in clear uptrend from tariff fears in April. Technical: $308 holding as support after pullback from $311 (Aug 5).
trades.catalyst: CPI report Aug 12 (binary but gold wins both ways); September rate cut 88.9% probability → weaker dollar; central bank demand structural; post-VIX-65 safe haven demand
trades.what_happened: CPI came in slightly below expectations (core +3.1% vs +3.0% est) on Aug 12 — market celebrated, rate cut odds surged, dollar weakened. GLD initially dipped slightly then recovered. The real move happened post-Aug 19: $305 → $308 → $315 → $325. Gold's breakout accelerated in late August as rate cut narrative solidified and dollar weakened. By Sep 5, GLD reached $331. The trade worked exactly as structured — the 'wins both ways' CPI setup proved correct.
trades.lesson: Macro setups where the asset wins under multiple scenarios are the best risk/reward trades. Gold in Aug 2025 was genuinely asymmetric: hawkish CPI = inflation hedge bid; dovish CPI = weaker dollar bid. Understanding the multi-path thesis separates high-conviction from directional bets.

trades.rationale: Cisco reported Q4 FY2025 on Aug 13: EPS $0.99 vs $0.98 (tiny beat), rev $14.7B vs $14.62B (tiny beat). AI orders $800M+ in Q4. BUT: stock fell -1.42% on the day despite the beat. 'Sell the news' dynamic. FY2026 guidance was merely inline with expectations (rev $59-60B, EPS $4.00-4.06). Key flaw: CSCO at $70 was trading at 17x FY2026 EPS — arguably full for a low-growth networking equipment vendor. The 'AI orders' narrative was priced in. Stock had run from $66 (Aug 5) to $70.60 (Aug 8) = +6% in 3 days before earnings. Now entering post-earnings drift lower.
trades.catalyst: Post-earnings 'sell the news'; inline FY2026 guidance disappointed after big run-up; AI orders narrative already priced; stock at 17x EPS for 8% revenue growth company; technical breakdown below $70
trades.what_happened: CSCO drifted lower after earnings: $69.23 → $68.15 → $65.10 (Aug 15!) → $65.84 → $65.65 (Aug 19) → $66-67 range through Aug 27. The post-earnings selling was real but modest — not a collapse. The stock found support at $65 (the pre-earnings level) and consolidated. Not a dramatic short but the thesis played out. Total decline from $70.60 peak to $65.10 = -7.8%. Entry was not at peak so captured only partial move.
trades.lesson: Post-earnings 'sell the news' shorts on low-growth, fully-valued names work but are low-conviction size trades. CSCO's AI narrative created a temporary 6% uplift that fully reversed within 2 weeks. Better to position SHORT on the pre-earnings run-up day (Aug 8 at $70.60) with a stop above earnings high.

trades.rationale: Google at $201.51 on Aug 13. CPI beat on Aug 12 lifted all mega-cap tech. GOOGL had Q2 results in July (strong cloud, search advertising resilient). The stock had been lagging the broader Mag-7 recovery — NVDA/META/AMZN all outperformed. September rate cut catalyst coming. More importantly: a major DOJ antitrust ruling expected to create 'sell the news' fear but with potential for positive resolution. Cloud growth 28%+ YoY was structural. GOOGL at 22x NTM earnings = cheapest Mag-7 name. Value within growth.
trades.catalyst: Cheapest Mag-7 on P/E basis; cloud segment 28%+ YoY growth; rate cut tailwind; laggard within Mag-7 = catch-up trade; DOJ antitrust fears creating artificial discount
trades.what_happened: GOOGL ground higher from $201 to $203, $205, $208, $211 through Aug 28. Then on Sep 3, a massive gap to $230 (+8.7% in one day) — likely tied to a positive DOJ antitrust ruling or major product announcement. By Sep 5 reached $234.47. The 14-day window captured a modest $201→$211 move (+4.65%) but the REAL move came the day after exit. The DOJ catalyst that was feared became a buying catalyst.
trades.lesson: When a stock is cheap within its peer group AND has a negative overhang that is more 'fear vs. reality' (DOJ ruling), the post-resolution trade can be explosive. Should have held GOOGL longer — the catalyst was identifiable. The Sep 3 jump to $230 was one of the biggest moves of the period. Exit timing cost significant alpha.

trades.rationale: Disney reported Q3 FY2025 on Aug 6: EPS beat $1.61 vs $1.45, but REVENUE MISS — first revenue miss since May 2024. Stock dropped from $117.52 (Aug 5) to $112 by Aug 7-8. Theme parks strong but the revenue miss signal suggests consumer slowdown reaching premium entertainment. Streaming subscriber growth decelerating. DIS was an 'overcrowded long' — analysts uniformly bullish on streaming recovery. Post-earnings weakness showed the bull thesis was already priced. At $115 on Aug 14, stock stabilizing after initial drop but fundamental thesis now questioned.
trades.catalyst: First revenue miss in over a year; consumer spending fatigue hitting theme parks discretionary pricing; streaming growth deceleration; DIS at $115 = modest premium to fair value given FCF trajectory; 'crowded long' positioning being unwound
trades.what_happened: DIS oscillated between $111-$118 range throughout the period. The revenue miss created a headwind but not a collapse. Theme park strength (actual segment performance was solid) provided support. Stock bounced from $111.67 (Aug 8) back to $115-118 range. The short thesis was directionally right (stock never recovered to $117+ pre-earnings levels) but within a 2-week window the stock was essentially flat, and the bounce to $118 on Aug 22 hit the stop-loss level. No meaningful decline materialized.
trades.lesson: DIS was a weak short — it broke from $117 to $111 already. The 'best' short was pre-earnings or on the Aug 6 gap-down to $114 (day of earnings). Post-gap shorts on consumer discretionary companies with 'mixed' (not catastrophic) earnings lack strong continuation. The revenue miss needed to compound over multiple quarters to create a structural short thesis.

trades.rationale: Coinbase at $317.55 on Aug 15. Bitcoin and crypto recovering with broader risk-on. Robinhood Q2 crypto revenue +98% YoY to $160M signals continued retail crypto engagement. Coinbase reported strong Q2 earlier (Jul 30): revenue +45% to $989M, EPS $0.42 vs $0.31 est. The Bitstamp acquisition ($200M) expanding international reach. Post-carry-trade crash crypto was temporarily hit; now recovering. Rate cut expectations supportive of speculative assets. COIN at $317 = 3% pullback from $327 peak (Aug 13). Technical momentum intact.
trades.catalyst: Crypto sector recovery post-VIX-65 spike; COIN Q2 beat (rev +45%, EPS +36% vs est); Bitstamp acquisition completed; rate cut September bets = risk-on; BTC recovering; HOOD crypto +98% signal cross-confirming retail demand
trades.what_happened: COIN peaked at $327 (Aug 13) and was already rolling over by Aug 15 when the trade was entered. It fell to $302 by Aug 19, $304 by Aug 21. The crypto sector faced selling pressure in the Aug 19-21 period coinciding with broader market softness. While the medium-term thesis was right (COIN reached $342 by Sep 18), the 10-day window captured the weak part of the cycle. Entry timing was poor — chasing a name that had already run hard.
trades.lesson: Crypto-correlated stocks have high volatility that requires timing discipline. COIN at $317 was already near-term extended — the right entry was the Aug 19-21 dip to $300-304. The thesis (crypto recovery, strong fundamentals, rate cut tailwind) was correct for the 6-week horizon but wrong for the 2-week window entered at peak.

trades.rationale: Walmart at $100.10 on Aug 18. Walmart reported strong Q1 results in May (+4.6% comps) and would report Q2 on Aug 21. Risk: bar is HIGH given Walmart's strong YTD performance. The stock ran from $98 (Aug 5 post-crash) to $103 by Aug 11 — a 5% bounce faster than the market. At $100, Walmart trades at ~28x earnings — expensive for a low-margin grocer even accounting for ecommerce mix shift. Consumer tariff impact on Walmart: it passes costs on, but higher prices = volume risk. Short into the Aug 21 earnings announcement as 'sell the news' on a priced-to-perfection name.
trades.catalyst: Priced-to-perfection valuation at 28x EPS; earnings Aug 21 = 'sell the news' risk; tariff pass-through risk to volumes; stock at 52-week highs; defensive stocks underperform in risk-on recovery
trades.what_happened: Walmart reported Q2 Aug 21: results were strong (ecommerce +25%, US comps +4.6%, raised FY2026 guidance). BUT the stock sold off: from $100 pre-earnings down to $97.37 on Aug 21, continuing to $96.25 on Aug 22, $95.50 by Aug 25. The 'sell the news' thesis worked even on a genuine beat — WMT was priced for perfection and the good news was insufficient. By Sep 1 at $97.27, captured a clean 2.8% short.
trades.lesson: Even genuine earnings beats can produce 'sell the news' reactions when valuation is extended and the stock has pre-run. WMT at 28x is expensive in any environment. Defensive names should underperform during risk-on recovery; they had their moment of strength during the Aug 5 panic. Now risk appetite improving = rotate out of defensives.

2025-08-06.market: SPY +0.77% ($627.46). Earnings heavy: DIS, UBER, ABNB, SHOP all reported. SMCI crashed -18% on double miss. SHOP gapped +22% on massive beat. Market recovering from VIX-65 Aug 5 spike. Japan carry trade unwind fears subsiding.
key_events: UBER earnings beat + $20B buyback but stock -0.2%
ABNB beat + $6B buyback; stock initially flat then fell next day
ideas_evaluated: LONG SHOP on gap (entered, see T01)
SHORT SMCI post-crash (entered, see T02)
LONG UBER on buyback dip (entered, see T04)
LONG TSLA recovery trade (entered, see T03)

2025-08-07.market: SPY -0.08% ($626.93). Market digested earnings day. Tariffs from Trump White House went live (extended pause expired). Initial risk-on then fade.
key_events: AMD partial recovery +5.7% after -6.4% prev day
ABNB continued lower -7.5% on guidance concerns
ideas_evaluated: LONG AMD on overreaction entry (entered, see T05)
AVOID ABNB short — gap already occurred

2025-08-08.market: SPY +0.78% ($631.82). CSCO +2.7% pre-earnings momentum. AMZN +0.6%. QQQ +0.96%.
key_events: CSCO surged ahead of Aug 13 earnings on AI narrative
ideas_evaluated: Monitor CSCO run-up for pre-earnings short setup (to execute post-earnings)

2025-08-11.market: SPY -0.20% ($630.57). Slight consolidation. TSLA +2.8% to $339 — notable outperformer.
key_events: TSLA strong momentum — autonomy/FSD narrative building
Jobs report weaker than expected — September cut odds firm
PLTR starting to show weakness after post-earnings run
ideas_evaluated: TSLA holding strong — consider adding if T03 in profit
PLTR setup forming for short entry

2025-08-12.market: SPY +1.07% ($637.28). CPI released: +0.2% MoM, +2.7% YoY (in-line/slightly below est). September cut odds surge to 88.9%. QQQ +1.27%. Strong day.
key_events: July CPI released: core +3.1% — in-line, tariff impact modest
META +3.2% to $788 — strong AI spending narrative
ideas_evaluated: SHORT PLTR at peak (T06 entered)
LONG GLD on CPI catalyst (T07 entered)
LONG META — considered but already +3.2% intraday, pass

2025-08-13.market: SPY +0.34% ($639.47). CSCO reported Q4 after close Aug 13 — beats but inline guidance, stock -1.4%.
key_events: CSCO Q4: EPS $0.99 beat, rev $14.7B beat, AI orders >$800M; FY26 guidance inline → stock fell -1.42%
AMD surged +6.7% to $184.42 (best day of the period) — institutional accumulation on data center thesis
ideas_evaluated: SHORT CSCO post-earnings (T08 entered)
LONG GOOGL (T09 entered at $201.51 — laggard catch-up)
Consider AMD add — but already +6.7% intraday; pass

key_events: AMZN +2.8% to $230 — AWS growth re-acceleration narrative
ideas_evaluated: SHORT DIS — bearish thesis post-revenue miss (T10 entered)
AMZN strength — considered long but already moved

2025-08-15.market: SPY -0.23% ($638.03). CSCO fell sharply to $65.10 (-4.5%) — post-earnings selling accelerating.
ideas_evaluated: LONG COIN on pullback (T11 entered — poorly timed)
Monitor CSCO T08 — working well

2025-08-18.market: SPY -0.02% ($637.89). Very quiet. Pre-Walmart earnings consolidation.
key_events: WMT stable at $100 ahead of Aug 21 earnings
UBER strong +1.5% to $93.98 — buyback bid evident
ideas_evaluated: SHORT WMT pre-earnings (T12 entered)
UBER T04 working — hold

2025-08-19.market: SPY -0.54% ($634.43). Broad mild selloff. Tech leading down. PLTR -9.4% to $157.75 (T06 winner). SMCI -5.7% to $43.24.
key_events: PLTR -9.4% single day — massive confirmation of T06 short thesis
Tech broad selling: NVDA -3.5%, AMD -5.4%, META -2%
ideas_evaluated: T06 PLTR short: consider covering some — already +15.5% in 7 days
T02 SMCI: another leg down confirms structural weakness

period_summary.macro_regime: Post-VIX-65 recovery with divergent stock picking. Market headline up (SPY +1.9% from $622.69 Aug 5 to $634.43 Aug 19) but significant cross-sectional dispersion. Winners: quality beats + recovery trades + rate-cut-correlated assets (Gold, TSLA). Losers: post-earnings gap-up chasers + extended high-multiple AI names (PLTR) + China-exposed semis.

trade_scorecard.best_trade: T06 SHORT PLTR +15.99% — post-peak AI valuation exhaustion short
trade_scorecard.worst_trade: T01 LONG SHOP -9.07% — chasing 22% gap-up on earnings day

key_patterns_observed.evidence: SHOP gapped +22% → immediately faded (-10% over 2 weeks). SMCI crashed -18% → further -5% over 2 weeks. AMD gapped -6% → partial recovery then re-failed. The initial gap move is the full move — entering after is usually wrong.
key_patterns_observed.rule: For earnings gap-ups >15%: wait for 3-5 day consolidation and re-test of gap support before entering long. For shorts: best entry is PRE-earnings on run-up, not post-crash.

key_patterns_observed.evidence: UBER announced $20B buyback (21% of market cap) → stock consistently ground higher despite initial sell-the-news. Buybacks on quality businesses with strong cash generation create persistent bid floors.
key_patterns_observed.rule: Large buyback authorizations (>15% of market cap) on profitable businesses with positive earnings momentum = high-conviction long with defined risk.

key_patterns_observed.evidence: TSLA +6.8% in first 5 days of period (vs SPY +1.9%). PLTR +8.1% from VIX-65 low to Aug 12 peak. High-beta names amplify both the crash AND the recovery. But then they become the shorts once momentum stalls.
key_patterns_observed.rule: On day 1-3 of post-VIX-spike recovery, size into high-beta quality names (TSLA, not junk). By day 7-10, flip to shorting the extended momentum plays (PLTR, SMCI).

key_patterns_observed.evidence: July CPI inline → September rate cut bets surged → dollar weakened → GLD grinded from $308 to $325+ over 3 weeks. The 'wins under multiple scenarios' thesis executed perfectly.
key_patterns_observed.rule: When an asset has positive asymmetry under multiple macro outcomes, size up with conviction. Gold in a rate-cut cycle with tariff inflation risk is the canonical example.

key_patterns_observed.pattern: Sell the News on Priced-in Beats
key_patterns_observed.evidence: CSCO beat EPS/rev but guidance was 'inline' → stock -7.8% from peak over 2 weeks. WMT beat comps/raised guidance → stock -3% day of earnings. UBER $20B buyback + beat → stock flat/down day of announcement.
key_patterns_observed.rule: When a stock has run 5-10% pre-earnings on narrative, even genuine beats fail if forward guidance is only 'inline'. The disappointment is in the guidance delta, not the reported quarter. Short stocks with 5%+ pre-earnings run that lack guidance upgrade catalyst.

key_patterns_observed.evidence: PLTR peaked exactly 8 trading days post-earnings (+21% from $154 to $187). Then fell -15.5% in 7 days. SHOP peaked day-of-earnings gap, then -10% over 10 days. Post-earnings momentum in overbought names tends to exhaust within 5-10 trading days.
key_patterns_observed.rule: For AI/high-momentum names, the post-earnings 'honeymoon' lasts 5-8 trading days max. After that, fundamentals and valuation re-assert. Short setup window: day 8-10 post-earnings when RSI >75 and no new catalyst.

missed_opportunities.setup: Sep 3 gap to $230 from $210 (+9%) — DOJ antitrust ruling positive catalyst. Should have held T09 GOOGL through this. Entry at $201 exit at $210 — left $20 on the table.
missed_opportunities.lesson: When shorting a stock's DOJ overhang via 'cheap within peer group' long, the resolution trade is the fat pitch. Exit too early = missed +9% in one day.

missed_opportunities.setup: Sep 11-19 TSLA ran from $335 to $426 (+27%) on autonomy/Cybercab catalysts. T03 exited at $351 (Aug 26) — missed a massive second leg.
missed_opportunities.lesson: TSLA autonomy story was the key risk for the fall. Should have used a trailing stop or partial profit-take rather than fixed exit date.

missed_opportunities.setup: AMZN went from $213 (Aug 5) to $231 (Aug 15) = +8.3% quietly with minimal headline. Clean post-crash recovery in a quality name with AWS reacceleration.
missed_opportunities.lesson: Don't overcomplicate — the simplest trades (buy quality mega-cap in recovery) often have the best risk/reward. AMZN's AWS thesis was the clearest structural story but got overlooked for more complex setups.

agent_conclusions: The period was a textbook 'recovery from panic' playbook: buy high-quality companies with momentum, short high-multiple names that front-ran good news, and own rate-cut-beneficiaries (GLD, long-duration).

The biggest edge in this period: correctly identifying post-earnings exhaustion in high-multiple AI names (PLTR short). The risk/reward on shorting PLTR at 90x earnings with no near-term catalyst after a +21% post-earnings run was exceptional.
The biggest mistake: chasing gap-ups on earnings day (SHOP). The entry thesis was fundamentally correct (massive beat) but the entry price (already +22%) eliminated margin of safety.

CPI day (Aug 12) was the pivot date of the period. September rate cut confirmation changed the entire calculus: risk-on accelerated, gold bid, dollar weakened, long-duration bonds eventually rallied. Correctly positioning BEFORE CPI on GLD was the cleanest macro trade.

Post-VIX-65 recovery divergence: quality companies with real earnings (AMZN, GOOGL, UBER) recovered steadily. Narrative/momentum plays (PLTR, SMCI, SHOP) either overshot on gap-up or continued lower. The 'quality sorts up' dynamic was strongly evident.
